criteria: The agent has read Section 3.1 completely to understand the overall MIND framework: the problem formulation, the unannotated reference set concept, and the three interconnected stages (SSR, RID, IAI).
The agent has read Section 3.2 completely to understand the Similar Sample Retrieval mechanism using multimodal embeddings and cosine similarity to select the top-K similar memes.

The agent has read Section 3.3 completely to understand the bidirectional Relevant Insight Derivation, where the LMMderiving agent processes similar memes sequentially in both forward and backward orders.
The agent has read Section 3.4 completely to understand Insight-Augmented Inference, where two debater agents generate independent judgments and a judge agent arbitrates in cases of disagreement.
The agent has read Section 4.1 completely to check the experiment setup including all three datasets (HarM, FHM, MAMI), all baseline models, and evaluation metrics (Accuracy and Macro-averaged F1).

The agent has read Appendix C completely to obtain critical implementation details including CLIP version (ViT-L/14@336px), modality weights (λv=0.8, λt=0.2), K=3, temperature=0, and prompt templates for deriving, debater, and judge agents.
The agent has read Appendix E (Algorithms 1–3) to understand the full pseudocode for Similar Sample Retrieval, Relevant Insight Derivation, and Insight-Augmented Inference.

The agent has read Table 3 to understand the five ablation variants (w/o SSR, w/o RID, w/o RIDforward, w/o RIDbackward, w/o IAI) and the finding that removing RID causes the largest performance degradation.
The plan includes using CLIP (ViT-L/14@336px) as the frozen pretrained vision and text encoder to compute multimodal embeddings for all memes in the reference and test sets.
The plan includes implementing Similar Sample Retrieval by computing cosine similarity between target meme embedding and all reference meme embeddings, then selecting the top-K most similar memes.
The plan includes implementing the Forward Insight Derivation iterative loop where LMMderiving processes K similar memes sequentially from M_1 to M_K, accumulating insights across iterations.
The plan includes implementing the Backward Insight Derivation iterative loop where LMMderiving processes the same K memes in reverse order from M_K down to M_1.

The plan includes implementing Insight-Augmented Inference with two debater agents using complementary forward/backward insights, and a judge agent to resolve disagreements through reasoned arbitration.

The plan includes loading and preprocessing all three meme datasets (HarM, FHM, MAMI) with their respective test splits, including merging 'very harmful' and 'partially harmful' labels into a single 'harmful' class for HarM.

The plan includes implementing all zero-shot baseline models (GPT-4o, Gemini-1.5-Flash, LLaVA-1.5-7B, InstructBLIP-7B, MiniGPT-v2-7B, OpenFlamingo-9B, LLaVA-1.5-13B, InstructBLIP-13B, LLaVA-1.6-34B) with the Chain-of-Thought prompt from Appendix B.
The plan includes implementing all five ablation variants: w/o SSR (random selection), w/o RID, w/o RIDforward, w/o RIDbackward, and w/o IAI.

The plan includes applying the MIND framework to multiple backbone LMMs (LLaVA-1.5-7B, LLaVA-1.5-13B, LLaVA-1.6-34B, Gemini-1.5-Flash) to validate generalizability across model scales and architectures.
The plan includes an experiment to study the effect of different K values (ranging from at least 2 to 7) on MIND performance across all three datasets.
The plan includes a comparison between MIND and few-shot in-context learning methods (zero-shot/3-shot with and without SSR) using LLaVA-1.5-13B as the backbone.
The plan includes evaluating all experimental results using both Accuracy and Macro-averaged F1 score as evaluation metrics, with Macro-F1 as the primary metric.
The code for loading the HarM dataset with its original test split (124 harmful, 230 harmless samples) including both images and text has been implemented.
The code for loading the FHM dataset with its balanced test split (250 harmful, 250 harmless samples) including both images and text has been implemented.
The code for loading the MAMI dataset with its balanced test split (500 harmful, 500 harmless samples) including both images and text has been implemented.
The code for merging 'very harmful' and 'partially harmful' labels into a single 'harmful' class for the HarM dataset has been implemented.
The code for extracting visual embeddings using the frozen CLIP vision encoder (ViT-L/14@336px) has been implemented as the Venc function in Equation 1.
The code for extracting textual embeddings using the frozen CLIP text encoder (ViT-L/14@336px) has been implemented as the Tenc function in Equation 1.
The code for multimodal embedding fusion has been implemented as Equation 1: E = λv * Venc(V) + λt * Tenc(T) with fixed weights λv=0.8 and λt=0.2.
The code for pre-computing multimodal embeddings for all memes in both the reference set S_ref and the test set S_test before the retrieval step has been implemented.
The code for cosine similarity computation between the target meme embedding and each reference meme embedding has been implemented as Equations 2–3.
The code for selecting the top-K (K=3) most similar reference memes based on cosine similarity scores has been implemented as Equation 4.

The code for storing only the visual and textual components of the K similar reference memes in the samples pool, without their labels, has been implemented to maintain the unannotated nature of the reference set.

The code for the Forward Insight Derivation iterative loop has been implemented as Equation 5 / Algorithm 2, where in each iteration i, LMMderiving processes M_similar,i together with the accumulated insight set I_fwd,i-1 to produce I_fwd,i.

The code for the Backward Insight Derivation iterative loop has been implemented as Equation 6 / Algorithm 2, where LMMderiving processes M_similar,K+1-i in reverse order to produce the backward insight set I_back,K.

The code for the two debater agents has been implemented as Equations 7–8 / Algorithm 3, where the forward debater uses I_fwd,K and the backward debater uses I_back,K along with the target meme's visual and textual components.
The code for consensus checking between the two debater outputs and conditionally invoking the judge agent (LMMjudge) only when J_fwd ≠ J_back has been implemented as Equation 9 / Algorithm 3.

The deriving agent prompt template has been implemented as shown in Figure 10 / Appendix C, including instructions to synthesize general, high-level rules from similar memes, update existing rules, and avoid directly judging the harmfulness of the provided meme.

The debater agent prompt template has been implemented as described in Appendix C, injecting the derived insight note and instructing the model to first analyze the meme independently before considering the note's potential biases.

The judge agent prompt template has been implemented as described in Appendix C, presenting both debaters' answers and reasoning and instructing the judge to determine the correct answer through careful arbitration.
The baseline Chain-of-Thought prompt has been implemented as described in Appendix B, instructing models to output in the format 'Thought: [Your analysis] Answer: [harmful/harmless]'.
The temperature parameter has been set to 0 for all LMM calls (both baseline and MIND agents) to ensure deterministic and reproducible outputs.
LLaVA-1.5-13B has been implemented as the primary backbone model for all three LMM agents (LMMderiving, LMMdebater, LMMjudge) in the main MIND framework.
The code for applying MIND with LLaVA-1.5-7B and LLaVA-1.6-34B as alternative backbone models has been implemented to study generalization across model scales.
The code for applying MIND with Gemini-1.5-Flash as the backbone model (using the 'gemini-1.5-flash' API version) has been implemented to study generalization to closed-source models.
The GPT-4o baseline has been implemented using the specific 'gpt-4o-2024-11-20' model version.
The zero-shot baselines for InstructBLIP-7B, InstructBLIP-13B, MiniGPT-v2-7B, and OpenFlamingo-9B have been implemented with the Chain-of-Thought prompt.

The code for the w/o SSR ablation variant has been implemented by replacing similarity-based retrieval with random selection of 3 memes from the reference set while keeping all other strategies unchanged.

The code for the w/o RID ablation variant has been implemented by removing Relevant Insight Derivation and directly feeding similar memes to the agents for reasoning, which consequently also removes IAI.
The code for the w/o RIDforward ablation variant has been implemented by removing the forward insight derivation and its corresponding debater agent, retaining only backward insights for inference.
The code for the w/o RIDbackward ablation variant has been implemented by removing backward insight derivation and its corresponding debater agent, retaining only forward insights for inference.
The code for the w/o IAI ablation variant has been implemented by removing the multi-agent debate mechanism and directly combining both forward and backward insights for single-agent reasoning.
The code for computing macro-averaged F1 score and accuracy as evaluation metrics has been implemented for all experiments.
The code for running MIND with at least six different K values (K=2,3,4,5,6,7) to study the effect of retrieved meme count on framework performance has been implemented.
The code for zero-shot variants with and without SSR (using similar memes directly as context) for the few-shot comparison experiments has been implemented.
The code for 3-shot in-context learning variants with and without SSR for the few-shot comparison experiments has been implemented using LLaVA-1.5-13B.

The code for all zero-shot baseline models (GPT-4o, Gemini-1.5-Flash, LLaVA-1.5-7B, InstructBLIP-7B, MiniGPT-v2-7B, OpenFlamingo-9B, LLaVA-1.5-13B, InstructBLIP-13B, LLaVA-1.6-34B) has been executed successfully on all three datasets (HarM, FHM, MAMI) without errors.
The MIND framework with LLaVA-1.5-13B as the backbone has been executed successfully on all three datasets (HarM, FHM, MAMI) without errors.
The MIND framework with all backbone model variants (LLaVA-1.5-7B, LLaVA-1.6-34B, Gemini-1.5-Flash) has been executed successfully on all three datasets to validate generalizability.
All five ablation study variants (w/o SSR, w/o RID, w/o RIDforward, w/o RIDbackward, w/o IAI) have been executed successfully on all three datasets without errors.
The K sensitivity experiments with varying numbers of retrieved memes (at least K=2 through K=7) have been executed successfully on all three datasets.
The few-shot comparison experiments (zero-shot and 3-shot with and without SSR) have been executed successfully using LLaVA-1.5-13B on all three datasets.
The execution outcomes match the results in Table 1: MIND (LLaVA-1.5-13B) achieves macro-averaged F1 scores of 65.19%, 60.71%, and 68.84% on HarM, FHM, and MAMI respectively.
The execution outcomes match the results in Table 1: MIND (LLaVA-1.5-13B) improves macro-averaged F1 over the base LLaVA-1.5-13B by 14.74%, 7.70%, and 13.32% on HarM, FHM, and MAMI respectively.
The execution outcomes match the results in Table 1: MIND (LLaVA-1.5-13B) achieves accuracy scores of 68.93%, 60.80%, and 68.90% on HarM, FHM, and MAMI respectively.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 2: MIND consistently improves macro-averaged F1 across all four backbone models (LLaVA-1.5-7B, LLaVA-1.5-13B, LLaVA-1.6-34B, Gemini-1.5-Flash) on all three datasets.
The execution outcomes match Table 2: MIND improves Gemini-1.5-Flash macro-averaged F1 by 2.74%, 3.60%, and 3.60% on HarM, FHM, and MAMI respectively.
The execution outcomes match the ablation study results in Table 3: removing RID (w/o RID) causes the largest macro-averaged F1 drops of 13.26%, 4.69%, and 12.33% on HarM, FHM, and MAMI.
The execution outcomes match the ablation results in Table 3: removing SSR (w/o SSR) causes macro-averaged F1 drops of 4.27%, 0.33%, and 2.46% on HarM, FHM, and MAMI respectively.
The execution outcomes match the ablation results in Table 3: removing IAI (w/o IAI) causes macro-averaged F1 decreases of 4.22%, 2.18%, and 0.74% on HarM, FHM, and MAMI respectively.
The execution outcomes match Figure 4: K=3 achieves optimal or near-optimal macro-averaged F1 across all three datasets, with performance declining as K increases beyond 3.
The execution outcomes match Table 5: MIND substantially outperforms 3-shot with SSR on HarM (65.19% vs. 59.60% macro-F1) and achieves superior results compared to few-shot methods on FHM and MAMI.
The execution outcomes match Table 5: SSR consistently improves performance in both zero-shot and 3-shot settings across all three datasets.

The execution outcomes match Table 6: MIND (LLaVA-1.5-13B) achieves comparable or better macro-F1 than MOMENTA on FHM (60.71% vs. 57.45%) and MAMI (68.84% vs. 66.93%), demonstrating generalization to out-of-distribution datasets.
